prevalence.population: Brazilian population (varies by region and sanitation)
prevalence.source: Parasitology surveillance syntheses in Latin America; WHO intestinal parasite context
prevalence.trend: decreasing overall with sanitation improvements; persistent hotspots in vulnerable communities

incidence.value: Variable; outbreaks/seasonal increases in high-risk settings
incidence.source: Public health surveillance and parasitology studies

burden.dalyPerCapita: low nationally; higher in endemic pockets due to dysentery and liver abscess
burden.mortalityRate: Low; higher in severe invasive disease without timely treatment

vulnerablePopulations: travelers and migrants from endemic zones
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical syndrome (diarrhea/dysentery) plus stool testing; antigen/PCR improves specificity vs microscopy; evaluate for amoebic liver abscess with imaging and serology when indicated

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no routine screening; testing based on symptoms/outbreaks
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Good with prompt therapy; reinfection risk persists where sanitation is poor

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Microscopy common; antigen/PCR more available in reference labs; imaging for liver abscess in tertiary care
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Metronidazole/tinidazole for invasive disease plus luminal agent (e.g., paromomycin) when available; oral rehydration and sanitation interventions
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Diagnostic limitations in primary care and persistent sanitation inequities in some regions
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Effective, low-cost therapy; strong impact of sanitation/clean water programs
